A modest capacity increase is the simplest next bet: one extra conv stage plus wider early channels and a slightly larger head, while leaving the optimizer side unchanged for now.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 2","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","primary_mode":"micro","secondary_modes":["topk"],"rationale":"With a fixed step budget, the simplest remaining lever is to increase the amount of data seen per update. Doubling the batch size should reduce gradient noise and expose more CIFAR-10 examples within the same 256-step run.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","primary_mode":"summaries","secondary_modes":["topk"],"rationale":"The short fixed-step budget is usually better served by a more aggressive schedule than plain cosine decay. A one-cycle policy should front-load adaptation and then anneal cleanly over the exact step budget.","edits":[{"op":"replace_function","function":"build_scheduler","old":"","new":"","anchor":"","text":"","source":"def build_scheduler(optimizer: optim.Optimizer, steps_per_epoch: int):
    if not USE_LR_SCHEDULE:
        return None
    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else steps_per_epoch * 100
    return optim.lr_scheduler.OneCycleLR(
        optimizer,
        max_lr=LEARNING_RATE,
        total_steps=max(1, total_steps),
        pct_start=0.3,
        anneal_strategy="cos",
        cycle_momentum=False,
        div_factor=10.0,
        final_div_factor=100.0,
    )"}]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","primary_mode":"micro","secondary_modes":["topk"],"rationale":"The run is still step-limited, so increasing batch size is the cleanest way to expose more CIFAR-10 examples per optimizer update. Moving to 256 should make each of the 256 steps substantially denser without changing the model or schedule.","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","primary_mode":"topk","secondary_modes":["summaries"],"rationale":"The model is now being trained with a larger batch and an explicit one-cycle schedule, so the next low-complexity move is to raise the peak learning rate. {"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","primary_mode":"indexing","secondary_modes":["summaries"],"rationale":"The last schedule change regressed, so the safe part to revert is the one-cycle shape that was already working best.
